refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refinement clarifies that availability is a measurable, time-based metric with explicit exclusions (planned maintenance) and inclusions (safety-driven events). High safety criticality demands that availability targets do not compromise safety protocols. The metric must be evaluated over a defined period to account for seasonal variations and maintenance cycles.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold) with automated calculation of availability ratio. Comparison against customer-defined baseline over rolling 30-day and annual periods. Root-cause analysis of downtime events categorized by failure mode (mechanical, electrical, software, environmental, safety-driven).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all state transitions (operational → non-operational) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall ensure safety-driven shutdowns do not degrade availability metric below customer-acceptable threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual sensors) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain uptime.
DP-B: Deploy predictive maintenance framework using telemetry analytics to forecast component failures before they occur, enabling proactive replacement and reducing mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP-C: Establish a modular architecture with hot-swappable components (battery packs, hydraulic modules, control units) to enable rapid field replacement without full system shutdown.
DP-D: Implement comprehensive health monitoring and diagnostics with automated alerts to maintenance teams, coupled with spare parts inventory management and logistics optimization.
DP-E: Combine DP-B (predictive maintenance) + DP-C (modular hot-swap) + DP-D (health monitoring) as an integrated availability strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Integrated Availability Strategy combining predictive maintenance analytics (DP-B), modular hot-swappable architecture (DP-C), and comprehensive health monitoring with automated alerting (DP-D). This multi-faceted approach addresses both prevention (predictive maintenance) and rapid recovery (modular swap), supported by real-time visibility (health monitoring), maximizing uptime while maintaining safety integrity.

potential_sources: Coupling between predictive maintenance algorithms and telemetry data quality: poor sensor calibration or data gaps will degrade prediction accuracy.
Coupling between modular architecture and system integration: hot-swap modules must maintain electrical/hydraulic/software interface compatibility; version mismatches could cause failures.
Coupling between health monitoring and safety systems: false negatives in diagnostics could mask safety-critical faults; false positives could trigger unnecessary shutdowns.
Coupling between spare parts logistics and field replacement capability: unavailable spares negate the benefit of modular design.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E is a composite strategy with internal dependencies. Each sub-component (B, C, D) is loosely coupled to the others but collectively addresses availability from multiple angles. The strategy assumes telemetry infrastructure exists and that field maintenance teams have training and tools for modular replacement. Coupling is manageable if interfaces are well-defined and data quality is assured.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, data pipeline, analytics platform) is available and reliable with ≥0.99 uptime.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance algorithms can achieve ≥0.85 precision (true positive rate) to avoid false alarms that erode operator trust.
ASSUMPTION: Modular components can be designed with standardized interfaces (electrical connectors, hydraulic quick-couplers, software APIs) without significant cost or weight penalties.
ASSUMPTION: Field maintenance teams will be trained and equipped to perform hot-swap operations safely within 30 minutes.
RISK: If predictive models are trained on historical data from different equipment variants or operating conditions, generalization may fail in new deployments.
RISK: Modular architecture may introduce additional failure modes (connector wear, seal degradation) that offset gains from replaceability.
RISK: Health monitoring system itself could become a single point of failure if not redundantly designed.
RISK: Spare parts inventory costs and logistics complexity may exceed budget if not carefully managed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-E to explicitly specify: (1) Redundancy strategy for health monitoring system itself (dual sensors, dual data paths); (2) Acceptance criteria for predictive model precision and recall; (3) Modular interface standards (electrical, hydraulic, software) with version compatibility rules; (4) Spare parts inventory policy (minimum stock levels, reorder triggers); (5) Field maintenance SOP with time budgets and safety protocols for hot-swap operations.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. DP-E (Integrated Availability Strategy) is realized across multiple modules: predictive maintenance analytics and health monitoring are primarily owned by Safety Services (diagnostics and alerting); modular architecture and hot-swap capability are primarily owned by Vehicle (mechanical/electrical design); telemetry data collection and transmission are shared between Vehicle and Teleoperation. The NGHE Solution IM boundary is the orchestrating container that ensures these modules work together to achieve the availability target.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle and Teleoperation emit continuous telemetry streams (sensor readings, state transitions, fault codes, performance metrics) to Safety Services and external monitoring systems. Telemetry is the data backbone for predictive maintenance and health monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services emit SafetyAlert messages to Site Manager, Tele-operator, and Maintenance teams when health monitoring detects anomalies or predictive models forecast imminent failures. Alerts trigger maintenance actions or operational restrictions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Site Manager and Tele-operator send Tasking commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation. Availability strategy must ensure Tasking is processed reliably; if health monitoring detects critical faults, Tasking may be rejected or constrained.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation and Safety Services communicate with remote monitoring/analytics platforms via Network Link. Predictive maintenance models may be updated remotely; health alerts may be escalated to cloud-based incident management systems.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the orchestrating container for DP-E. Availability strategy is realized across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but the NGHE Solution boundary ensures coherent integration, interface contracts, and end-to-end availability measurement. Minimal diffusion: the strategy is well-localized to these three modules with clear ownership.

Vehicle owns modular hot-swap architecture (DP-C) and contributes telemetry data collection (DP-D). However, Vehicle does not own predictive maintenance analytics (DP-B) or health monitoring logic, which reside in Safety Services. Diffusion arises because Vehicle must implement standardized interfaces and comply with health monitoring directives, but the intelligence is elsewhere.

Teleoperation contributes telemetry transmission and may relay health alerts to remote operators. However, Teleoperation does not own core availability mechanisms (predictive maintenance, modular design, health monitoring). High diffusion: Teleoperation is a conduit for availability-related data but not a primary owner of DP-E logic.

Safety Services owns predictive maintenance analytics (DP-B) and health monitoring logic (DP-D), including fault detection, alert generation, and diagnostics. Safety Services is a primary owner of DP-E. Diffusion arises because Safety Services depends on telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation, and must coordinate with Vehicle on maintenance actions.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (orchestrating container) with primary sub-ownership by Safety Services (predictive maintenance and health monitoring) and Vehicle (modular architecture and hot-swap capability). Teleoperation is a supporting module for telemetry transmission and alert relay.
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-E (Integrated Availability Strategy) is best realized as a distributed responsibility across NGHE Solution (orchestration), Safety Services (analytics and diagnostics), and Vehicle (mechanical/electrical design). The NGHE Solution boundary ensures that these modules are integrated coherently and that availability is measured end-to-end. No single module fully contains DP-E, but the distribution is logical and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: If Safety Services and Vehicle teams do not coordinate closely on interface standards and data contracts, predictive maintenance insights may not translate into actionable maintenance directives for Vehicle.
RISK: If telemetry data quality is poor (missing sensors, calibration drift, transmission delays), predictive models will degrade and availability targets will be missed.
RISK: If modular architecture introduces new failure modes (connector wear, seal leaks) that are not captured by health monitoring, actual availability may fall short of predictions.
RISK: If spare parts inventory is not managed at the NGHE Solution level, individual modules may hoard spares, leading to inefficient allocation and availability shortfalls.
RISK: If Teleoperation network link is unreliable, remote health monitoring and predictive model updates may be delayed, reducing effectiveness of DP-E.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a cross-module Availability Working Group (Safety Services, Vehicle, Teleoperation, System Engineering) to define: (1) Telemetry data schema and quality standards; (2) Interface contracts for health monitoring directives and maintenance actions; (3) Modular component interface standards (electrical, hydraulic, software); (4) Spare parts inventory policy and allocation rules; (5) End-to-end availability measurement methodology and reporting cadence. Assign explicit ownership of each sub-component of DP-E (predictive maintenance to Safety Services, modular design to Vehicle, health monitoring to Safety Services, spare parts logistics to Maintenance/Quality).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E (Integrated Availability Strategy) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI ≥0.97). The strategy combines prevention (predictive maintenance), rapid recovery (modular hot-swap), and visibility (health monitoring) to maximize uptime. The logic is sound: predictive maintenance reduces unplanned failures; modular architecture enables fast replacement; health monitoring provides early warning. Coupling risks are identified and manageable. DP-E is appropriate for high safety criticality because it emphasizes early detection and controlled maintenance rather than reactive repairs.

diffusion_risks: DP-E is distributed across NGHE Solution (orchestration), Safety Services (analytics), and Vehicle (design). Diffusion is moderate but acceptable because the distribution aligns with natural module boundaries and expertise.

Teleoperation has lower containment (0.40) but plays a supporting role in telemetry transmission and alert relay. Diffusion here is acceptable because Teleoperation is not responsible for core availability logic.

Risk of interface misalignment between Safety Services (health monitoring directives) and Vehicle (modular replacement actions). Mitigation: establish clear interface contracts and cross-module testing.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution (0.95 containment) is the appropriate orchestrating boundary. Safety Services (0.75 containment) and Vehicle (0.60 containment) are primary sub-owners. Teleoperation (0.40 containment) is a supporting module. The distribution is logical and minimizes unnecessary diffusion. No single module is overloaded; responsibilities are clear.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability is measurable via continuous telemetry logging and automated calculation of uptime ratio. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold); (2) root-cause analysis of downtime events; (3) comparison against customer-defined baseline over agreed evaluation period. Feasibility is high if telemetry infrastructure is reliable and data quality is assured.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of DP-E to NGHE Solution, Safety Services, and Vehicle is clear. Each module has explicit responsibilities: Safety Services owns predictive maintenance and health monitoring; Vehicle owns modular architecture and hot-swap capability; NGHE Solution orchestrates integration and measures end-to-end availability. Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-E to specify acceptance criteria for predictive model precision/recall and health monitoring false-positive rate.
Establish cross-module interface contracts (telemetry schema, health monitoring directives, maintenance action protocols) before detailed design.
Define spare parts inventory policy and allocation rules at the NGHE Solution level to ensure efficient resource utilization.
Implement redundancy for health monitoring system itself (dual sensors, dual data paths) to avoid single-point-of-failure.
Plan for field maintenance training and SOP development to ensure hot-swap operations are performed safely and efficiently.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) is a high-level, composite requirement that cannot be fully realized at a single level. DP-E (Integrated Availability Strategy) is sound and well-allocated to NGHE Solution, Safety Services, and Vehicle. However, DP-E comprises multiple sub-strategies (predictive maintenance, modular architecture, health monitoring) that require further decomposition into atomic FRs and DPs. The sub-FRs identified (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are appropriate entry points for the next decomposition level. Proceeding to the next level will clarify detailed design requirements, interface contracts, and implementation details.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all state transitions (operational → non-operational) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall ensure safety-driven shutdowns do not degrade availability metric below customer-acceptable threshold.

assumptions: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, data pipeline, analytics platform) is available and reliable with ≥0.99 uptime.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can achieve ≥0.85 precision to avoid false alarms.
Modular components can be designed with standardized interfaces without significant cost or weight penalties.
Field maintenance teams will be trained and equipped to perform hot-swap operations safely within 30 minutes.
Customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period.
Safety-driven shutdowns are acceptable and do not count as availability failures if properly logged and justified.
open_risks: Predictive model generalization: models trained on historical data may not generalize to new equipment variants or operating conditions.
Modular architecture failure modes: hot-swap connectors and seals may introduce new failure modes that offset gains from replaceability.
Health monitoring single-point-of-failure: if health monitoring system itself fails, availability visibility is lost.
Spare parts inventory costs and logistics complexity may exceed budget.
Teleoperation network link unreliability may delay remote health monitoring and model updates.
Interface misalignment between Safety Services (health monitoring directives) and Vehicle (modular replacement actions).
Field maintenance SOP and training may be inadequate, leading to slow or unsafe hot-swap operations.
evidence_needed: Telemetry infrastructure design and reliability analysis (MTBF, MTTR, data loss rate).
Predictive maintenance model validation results (precision, recall, false-positive rate) on representative datasets.
Modular architecture design with interface standards (electrical, hydraulic, software) and compatibility rules.
Health monitoring system design with redundancy and fault tolerance analysis.
Spare parts inventory policy and logistics cost-benefit analysis.
Field maintenance SOP with time budgets, safety protocols, and training plan.
End-to-end availability measurement methodology and baseline data from similar systems.
Customer acceptance criteria for availability target and evaluation period.
